coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of N,S co-doped carbon dots for fluorescence turn-on detection of Fe2+ and Al3+ in a wide pH range
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Yanxi
Xia, Xiaofeng
Xiao, Zhongwen
Zhao, Yong
Yan, Mengjie
Li, Jiayin
Li, Hongqi
Liu, Xiaotian
coredata.dc:description: N,S co-doped carbon dots (N/S-CDs) was synthesized by using β-cyclodextrin as carbon source and Reactive Black 5 as nitrogen and sulfur source. The structure of the N/S-CDs was characterized and its performance for detection of Fe2+ and Al3+ was evaluated. Experimental results demonstrated that the N/S-CDs had good stability in 10 mmol/L brine and in a wide pH range of 3–12. The N/S-CDs exhibited high selectivity for detection of Fe2+ and Al3+ by dual-modal fluorescence enhancement and visualization with the naked eye over a wide pH range. The N/S-CDs displayed good anti-interference performance and low detection limits of 6.99 nmol/L for Fe2+ and 5.5 nmol/L for Al3+. The N/S-CDs responded to Al3+ rapidly within 3 min. Measurements on particle size, zeta potential, and UV–vis and FT-IR spectra suggested that Fe2+ enhanced fluorescence of the N/S-CDs by chelating while Al3+ promoted aggregation through combination with the N/S-CDs which responded to Al3+ by aggregation-induced enhancement of emission (AIEE) mechanism.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
N,S-doping
Fluorescence enhancement
Sensor
Fe2+
Al3+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of the N/S-CDs to Fe2+ and Al3+
Detection limits of the N/S-CDs toward Fe2+ and Al3+

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering of Polymers
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering of Polymers

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The Funding for the Open Research Program of State Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering of Polymers, Fudan University (K2022-38 to Yanxi Song) is acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM, (b) Particle size, and (c) XRD of the N/S-CDs. (d) FT-IR spectra of β-CD, RB5 and the N/S-CDs.
(a) The full XPS spectrum, (b) C1s spectra, (c) S2p spectra, and (d) N1s spectra of the N/S-CDs.

(a) UV–vis spectra of β-CD, RB5 and the N/S-CDs. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of β-CD, RB5, H2O and the N/S-CDs. (c) Color of solution of the N/S-CDs, β-CD and RB5 and H2O in the sunlight. (d) Color of solution of the N/S-CDs, β-CD and RB5 and H2O under 365 nm UV light. (e) Fluorescence emission spectra of the N/S-CDs excited with different wavelength. (f) Fluorescence emission and excitation spectra of the N/S-CDs at the optimal excitation and emission wavelength.
(a) Fluorescence intensities of the N/S-CDs solutions of different pH (3–12). (b) Fluorescence intensities of the N/S-CDs in solutions of different ionic strength.

(a) Fluorescence emission of the N/S-CDs after addition of different cations (Li+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Mg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, Fe3+ and Al3+). (b) Color of solution of the N/S-CDs, the N/S-CDs + Fe2+, and the N/S-CDs + Al3+ under sunlight.

Fluorescence intensity of the N/S-CDs and (a) the N/S-CDs + Fe2+, (b) the N/S-CDs + Al3+ under different pH. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of (c) the N/S-CDs + Fe2+, (d) the N/S-CDs + Al3+ and equilibration time.
Fluorescence intensity of (a) the N/S-CDs, the N/S-CDs + Fe2+, and the N/S-CDs + Fe2+ + other cations, and (b) the N/S-CDs, the N/S-CDs + Al3+, and the N/S-CDs + Al3+ + other cations.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of the N/S-CDs containing different concentrations of Fe2+. (b) The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the N/S-CDs/Fe2+ system and concentration of Fe2+. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of the N/S-CDs containing different concentrations of Al3+. (d) The relationship between the fluorescence intensity of the N/S-CDs/Al3+ system and concentration of Al3+.
(a) Particle size of the N/S-CDs + Fe2+. (b) Zeta potential, (c) UV–vis spectra, and (d) FT-IR spectra of the N/S-CDs before and after adding Fe2+.
(a) Particle size of the N/S-CDs + Al3+. (b) Zeta potential, (c) UV–vis spectra, and (d) FT-IR spectra of the N/S-CDs before and after adding Al3+.
Illustration of design of N,S co-doped carbon dots for fluorescence turn-on detection of Fe2+ and Al3+ in a wide pH range.
Possible mechanism of Al3+ sensing by the N/S-CDs.
Comparison of performance of different fluorescent sensors for Fe2+ detection.
Comparison of performance of different fluorescent sensors for Al3+ detection.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of N,S co-doped carbon dots for fluorescence turn-on detection of Fe2+ and Al3+ in a wide pH range

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Donghua University, 2999 North Renmin Road, Shanghai 201620, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Donghua University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Donghua University, 2999 North Renmin Road, Shanghai 201620, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering & Biotechnology, Donghua University, 2999 North Renmin Road, Shanghai 201620, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Material Engineering, Huainan Normal University, 232038 Huainan, Anhui Province, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Material Engineering, Huainan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Material Engineering, Huainan Normal University, 232038 Huainan, Anhui Province, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N,S co-doped carbon dots (N/S-CDs) was synthesized from β-cyclodextrin (carbon source) and Reactive Black 5 (nitrogen and sulfur source). N/S-CDs exhibited high selectivity for Fe2+ and Al3+ by fluorescence enhancement and visualization, good anti-interference performance and low detection limits of 6.99 nmol/L for Fe2+ and 5.5 nmol/L for Al3+, rapid response to Al3+ by AIEE.

N,S co-doped carbon dots (N/S-CDs) was synthesized by using β-cyclodextrin as carbon source and Reactive Black 5 as nitrogen and sulfur source. The structure of the N/S-CDs was characterized and its performance for detection of Fe2+ and Al3+ was evaluated. Experimental results demonstrated that the N/S-CDs had good stability in 10 mmol/L brine and in a wide pH range of 3–12. The N/S-CDs exhibited high selectivity for detection of Fe2+ and Al3+ by dual-modal fluorescence enhancement and visualization with the naked eye over a wide pH range. The N/S-CDs displayed good anti-interference performance and low detection limits of 6.99 nmol/L for Fe2+ and 5.5 nmol/L for Al3+. The N/S-CDs responded to Al3+ rapidly within 3 min. Measurements on particle size, zeta potential, and UV–vis and FT-IR spectra suggested that Fe2+ enhanced fluorescence of the N/S-CDs by chelating while Al3+ promoted aggregation through combination with the N/S-CDs which responded to Al3+ by aggregation-induced enhancement of emission (AIEE) mechanism.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an essential trace element in organisms, iron played an important role in a variety of physiological processes in the body [1]. Abnormal concentrations of iron would cause harm to human body by inducing the dysfunction of liver, kidney and other organs. The abnormal iron concentration may also cause neurological diseases such as Parkinson’s and Alzheimer’s disease [2,3]. As the most abundant metal element in the earth’s crust, aluminum was often used in our daily life [4]. Excessive intake of Al3+ not only affected the aquatic environment, but also led to various diseases [5,6]. At present, there were a variety of technologies such as atomic absorption spectrum, high-efficiency liquid chromatography, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry and voltammetry for quantitative analysis of Al3+ or Fe2+ [7–10]. However, these methods usually required complex operations and cumbersome processes. By contrast, the fluorescence analysis methods had the advantages of rapid detection, simple operation and high selectivity and sensitivity for Fe2+ and Al3+ and other metal ions sensing. As a new type of functional material, carbon dots (CDs) had been converted to flourishing fluorescent nanosensors due to their advantages including nanosize, high water solubility, low cytotoxicity, tunable fluorescence, simple preparation, and surface functionalization flexibility [11–16]. More recently, increasing endeavors, mainly focused on doping with hetroatoms like N, S, B and P, were devoted to modifying CDs structures for improving the quantum yield and the optical and material properties of CDs to construct selective fluorogenic probes for sensing various metal ions [17–21] and other analytes [22–27].

Fluorescent probes based on CDs for detection of Fe2+ [28–36] or Al3+ [37–45] were also reported by researchers. Qin’s team [15] used anhydrous citric acid and 1,10-phenanthroline to synthesize CDs for detecting Fe2+ and Fe3+ by fluorescence quenching and for cell imaging [28]. Du et al. and Zhang group developed Fe2+ probe derived from S-doped CDs [29] and dopamine functionalized N,S co-doped CDs [30], respectively. However, the selectivity of these probes was not high and Fe2+ could not be distinguished from Fe3+. Rawat’s group obtained CDs from mango leaves by pyrolysis and found that Fe2+ could quench the fluorescence of the CDs with the detection limit reaching 0.622 pmol/L [31]. However, the CDs was unstable under strong acids and strong bases condition. On the other hand, fluorescent chemosensors based on N,S-doped CDs for selective detection of Al3+ has not been reported by far. Therefore, it is significant and challenging to develop CDs-based fluorogenic Fe2+ and Al3+ chemosensors with high selectivity and adaptability in a broad pH scale. We envisage to prepare nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots by using β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) as the carbon source and Reactive Black 5 (RB5) as the nitrogen and sulfur source through one-step hydrothermal method. The strategy is proposed based on the following considerations as illustrate in Scheme 1 . β-CD is selected as the carbon source for preparation of CDs because it can not only stabilize the nanoparticles but also enhance the molecular recognition property of the CDs [46]. RB5 is used in preparation of CDs because it can ensure sufficient N,S-doping. And a recent report revealed that the introduction of S atoms might promote the molecular fluorophore formation due to the increased N content in CDs [47]. Since RB5 is widely used in dyeing industry, this study may also provide a way for recovery and utilization of RB5-containing printing and dyeing wastewater [48]. The prepared N/S-CDs has abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface and can coordinate with Fe2+ and Al3+ thus enhance the fluorescence through chelation or Al3+-promoted aggregation-induced enhancement of emission (AIEE) [37]. Therefore the N/S-CDs can be used for dual fluorescent and visual detection of Fe2+ and Al3+ in aqueous solution under strongly acidic or alkaline conditions.

RB5 was purchased from Shanghai Lingfeng chemical reagent Co., Ltd and other reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). β-CD and RB5 were used as raw materials directly without further purification. The metal ion solutions for sensing experiments were prepared from the following metal salts: LiCl, NaCl, KCl, AgNO3, MgCl2·6H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, Pb(CH3COO)2, NiCl2·6H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, CaCl2, CrCl3·6H2O, Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O, MnSO4·H2O, CdCl2, FeSO4·7H2O, FeCl3·6H2O and Al2(SO4)3.

The morphology was observed on a FEI Talos F200S Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM). X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by a Thermofisher Escalab 250xi. X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) was obtained by using a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer. FT-IR spectra were measured by using a Nicolet iS10 Thermo Scientific Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer with an ATR unit. The particle size and zeta potential of the N/S-CDs were measured with a Brookhaven NanoBrook 90Plus PALS. UV–vis spectra were measured on a TU-1810APC Ultraviolet spectrophotometer. Fluorescence emission spectra were obtained by using a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The measurements were carried out under conditions of the excitation wavelength being 340 nm, the slit width being 5 nm, and the scanning range being 350–600 nm.

N/S-CDs was synthesized by the hydrothermal reaction as illustrated in Scheme 2 . The raw material β-CD (0.500 g) was added to 35 ml of deionized water and the mixture was submitted to ultrasonic treatment until β-CD was completely dissolved. Then RB5 (0.327 g) was added to the above solution and the mixture was stirred for 10 min. After the obtained solution was transferred to a reactor with polyethylene tetrafluoroethylene lining, the reactor was heated to 180 °C and maintained for 12 h. The reaction solution was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 30 min. The supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μm filtration membrane to afford a brown transparent N/S-CDs solution which was stored at 4 °C.

The morphology of the prepared CDs was observed by TEM as showed in Fig. 1 (a). It was visible that the N/S-CDs was basically spherical. The particle size of the N/S-CDs mainly concentrated at 3–5 nm as showed in Fig. 1(b). XRD spectrum of the N/S-CDs was measured and showed in Fig. 1(c). The (002) peak at 21.2° indicated the amorphous structure of the N/S-CDs. Fig. 1(d) showed the FT-IR spectra of β-CD, RB5 and the N/S-CDs. The broad band centered 3302 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H and N–H bonds. The broad peak at 2926 cm−1 was caused by C–H stretching vibration. The peak at 1696 cm−1 originated from the stretching vibration of C=O bond. The peak at 1152 cm−1 was caused by the stretching vibration of C–S, C–N, and C–O bonds. The sharp peak at 1020 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of C–O–C bonds. The peak at 864 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibration of the C–H bond while the peak at 756 cm−1 was caused by the bending vibration of C–S bond. The presence of these functional groups indicated that nitrogen and sulfur had been successfully doped into the carbon dots and the presence of hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl groups enabled the N/S-CDs to display good water solubility.

Fig. 2 (a) showed the full XPS spectrum of the N/S-CDs. The four peaks corresponded to O1s peak at 531.79 eV, N1s peak at 399.69 eV, C1s peak at 284.80 eV, and S2p peak at 168.48 eV, respectively. The C1s peak in Fig. 2(b) could be deconvoluted into three peaks, namely the peak at 284.73 eV which was attributed to C–C bond, the peak at 286.08 eV which was assigned to C–O/C–S/C–N bonds, and the peak at 288.14 eV which belonged to C=O/C=N bonds. The S2p peak in Fig. 2(c) was deconvoluted in two peaks. The one at 163.91 eV which was not very obvious might be attributed to C–S bond and the other at 168.58 eV was caused by S=O bond. Two peaks at 399.59 eV and 401.36 eV respectively were observed in the N1s spectra as showed in Fig. 2(d). They were corresponding to N–H and C–N bonds. These XPS data were basically consistent with the FT-IR spectral analysis, demonstrating the successful synthesis of the carbon dots and the successful doping of nitrogen and sulfur into the carbon dots.

In order to further verify the successful synthesis of the N/S-CDs, UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopic experiments were performed on the N/S-CDs together with the raw materials. UV–vis spectrum of the N/S-CDs was different from that of the raw materials β-CD and RB5 as showed in Fig. 3 (a), indicating the successful synthesis of the N/S-CDs. The absorption band centered 350 nm in UV–vis spectrum of the N/S-CDs was attributed to the n → π* electronic transition in C=O bond [34,49]. Fluorescence emission spectra of β-CD, RB5, H2O and the N/S-CDs were showed in Fig. 3(b). The spectra revealed that β-CD, RB5 and H2O were non-fluorescent while the N/S-CDs exhibited a strong and broad fluorescence emission band centered 434 nm. The synthesized N/S-CDs solution was brown, different from the color of any raw material as showed in Fig. 3(c). The N/S-CDs solution emitted light yellow fluorescence under the irradiation of 365 nm UV lamp, while the raw materials were non-fluorescent as showed in Fig. 3(d). The above results confirmed that the N/S-CDs was successfully synthesized.

In order to explore the optimal fluorescence excitation wavelength for the N/S-CDs, fluorescence emission spectra of the N/S-CDs excited with different wavelength (310, 320, 330, 340, 350, 360, 370 and 380 nm) were determined and showed in Fig. 3(e). It was clearly seen that the fluorescence intensity of the N/S-CDs reached the highest value when the excitation wavelength was 340 nm, as showed in Fig. 3(f).

Stability was one of the dominant properties of fluorescence materials because it was related to their application in real environments. So the effects of pH and ionic strength on the stability of the N/S-CDs were investigated. Fluorescence intensities of the N/S-CDs solutions of different pH (3–12) were showed in Fig. 4 (a). Under the condition of pH 3–9, the N/S-CDs maintained a high degree of stability and the fluorescence intensity was the highest at pH 7 which was set for subsequent fluorescence measurement experiments. Though the fluorescence intensity began to decrease gradually with increase of the pH value from 9 to 12, the fluorescence intensities maintained relatively high values in the whole pH test range. The decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the N/S-CDs under strongly alkaline conditions may be ascribed to existence of the large number of hydrophilic hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of the N/S-CDs, which undergo deprotonation under strongly basic conditions thus destroy the surface passivation and affect the fluorescence performance of the N/S-CDs [34,50]. Fluorescence intensities of the N/S-CDs in solutions of different ionic strength were showed in Fig. 4(b). It could be seen from the figure that adding different concentrations (1–10 mmol/L) of NaCl ion did not affect the stability of the N/S-CDs. These results demonstrated that the N/S-CDs had good salt resistance and was stable in a wide range of pH 3–12.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of the N/S-CDs to Fe2+ and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: The N/S-CDs and various metal ions were mixed in deionized water for fluorescence test and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded in Fig. 5 (a). It was visible that the fluorescence intensity of the N/S-CDs did not change substantially after adding Li+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Mg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cd2+ and Fe3+. Only the addition of Fe2+ and Al3+ could distinctly enhance the fluorescence intensity. Fig. 5(b) showed the color of solution of the N/S-CDs before and after adding Fe2+ and Al3+ under sunlight. It was clearly seen that the pure N/S-CDs solution turned out to be reddish-brown and transparent. The solution became brown and dark brown after the addition of Fe2+ and Al3+ respectively. These results demonstrated that the N/S-CDs was highly selective for the detection of Fe2+ and Al3+ and the two metal ions could be recognized by dual-mode of fluorescence enhancement and visualization by the naked eye.

In order to study the effect of different pH on the fluorescence detection of Fe2+ and Al3+, fluorescence emission spectra of solutions of different pH containing the N/S-CDs and Fe2+ or Al3+ were measured and recorded. Fig. 6 (a) showed the fluorescence intensity of the N/S-CDs before and after addition of Fe2+ at different pH (3–12). It was clear that the N/S-CDs could detect Fe2+ in the range of pH 5–12. Likewise, as showed in Fig. 6(b), the applicable pH range for detecting Al3+ by the N/S-CDs was pH 4–10. Taking into account the applicable conditions for actual samples detection, pH 7 was set for subsequent detection experiments. The response time of the N/S-CDs for detecting Fe2+ and Al3+ had been determined and showed in Fig. 6(c) and 6(d), respectively. The fluorescence intensity of the N/S-CDs increased with time upon addition of Fe2+ and exhibited remarkable enhancement in 20 min. Then the fluorescence intensity of the N/S-CDs continued to increase and after 55 min the fluorescence intensity was almost constant. Addition of Al3+ to the N/S-CDs induced rapid increase of the fluorescence intensity. About 3 min after the addition, the fluorescence intensity tended to become constant. The results manifested that the N/S-CDs responded to Fe2+ and Al3+ rapidly and the response time for Al3+ was much shorter than that for Fe2+.

The interference of competitive metal ions to the detection of Fe2+ and Al3+ by the N/S-CDs was investigated. Fig. 7 showed the fluorescence intensity of the N/S-CDs, the N/S-CDs plus Fe2+ or Al3+, and the N/S-CDs plus Fe2+ or Al3+ plus competitive cations. From Fig. 7(a) it could be seen that except for Al3+ which could significantly enhance the fluorescence intensity of the N/S-CDs, addition of other competitive metal ions including Li+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Mg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cd2+ and Fe3+ had no obvious affect on detection of Fe2+. Similarly, as could be found from Fig. 7(b), when different competitive metal ions were added to the mixed solution of the N/S-CDs and Al3+, only Fe2+ enhanced the fluorescence intensity of the mixture while other competitive metal ions had no substantial effect on the sensing of Al3+. The results demonstrated that the N/S-CDs could be used for simultaneous detection of Fe2+ and Al3+ with excellent anti-interference performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection limits of the N/S-CDs toward Fe2+ and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectra of the N/S-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe2+ and Al3+ were tested and recorded to investigate its potential applicability and limit of detection (LOD). Fig. 8 (a) showed the fluorescence emission spectra of the N/S-CDs after addition of different concentrations of Fe2+. With the increase of the concentration of Fe2+, the fluorescence intensity of the N/S-CDs increased gradually. The correlation of the fluorescence intensity with concentration of Fe2+ was reflected in Fig. 8(b). It was found that the fluorescence intensity had a good linear relationship with the concentration of Fe2+ with R2 = 0.9970. The LOD was calculated to be 6.99 nmol/L according to the formulae LOD = 3σ/S (σ represented standard deviation and S was the slope of the line). Similarly, the fluorescence intensity of the N/S-CDs ascended with the increase of Al3+ concentration as showed in Fig. 8(c). The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Al3+ showed in Fig. 8(d) was divided into two stages based on the range of Al3+ concentration. In the first stage with Al3+ concentration in 0–10 μmol/L range, the fluorescence intensity increased rapidly with increase of Al3+ concentration and the linear correlation coefficient was 0.9986. While in the second stage with Al3+ concentration in 12–40 μmol/L range, the fluorescence intensity rose slowly with increase of Al3+ concentration and a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Al3+ was obtained. The detection limit of the N/S-CDs for sensing Al3+ was calculated to be 5.50 nmol/L.

The mechanism of fluorescence enhancement of the N/S-CDs in the presence of Fe2+ or Al3+ ions was investigated by means of particle size, zeta potential value, UV–vis and FT-IR absorption spectral measurements. As showed in Fig. 9 (a-c), the particle size, zeta potential and UV–vis absorption spectrum of the N/S-CDs remained essentially unchanged after adding Fe2+. FT-IR spectra of the N/S-CDs before and after adding Fe2+ were displayed in Fig. 9(d). It could be found that the original absorption peak at 1696 cm−1 in the N/S-CDs assignable to the stretching vibration of C=O bond moved to 1680 cm−1 after adding Fe2+, demonstrating the impact of Fe2+ addition on the carboxyl group. We speculated that the mechanism of fluorescence enhancement of the N/S-CDs by Fe2+ ions was as follows. The N/S-CDs contained functional groups including electron-donating amino and hydroxyl groups and electron-withdrawing carboxyl groups on the surfaces. Fe2+ chelated with the carboxyl groups on the surfaces of the N/S-CDs and reduced the electron-accepting ability of the carboxyl groups, leading to increase in the possiblity of electron-hole recombination and resulted in fluorescence enhancement [34,51].

The particle size of the N/S-CDs after adding Al3+ was showed in Fig. 10 (a). Compared with the particle size of the N/S-CDs as displayed in Fig. 1(b), the particle size of the N/S-CDs after addition of Al3+ increased by 7–8 folds. Meanwhile the zeta potential value of the N/S-CDs decreased from 30.23 mV to 13.54 mV upon addition of Al3+ ions as showed in Fig. 10(b). It was known that the larger was the size of the dispersed particles, the lower was the zeta potential of the particles, the more prone to form the coagulation state were the particles. Therefore, the increase in the size and the decrease in the zeta potential of the N/S-CDs after addition of Al3+ indicated that the addition of Al3+ caused the formation of aggregation from the N/S-CDs, thereby enhanced the fluorescence through the so-called aggregation-induced enhancement of emission (AIEE) [37]. Thus the as-prepared N/S-CDs was expected to be used as an AIEE luminescent material through Al3+ functionalization. Fig. 10(c) revealed the slight change in 340–420 nm region of the UV–vis spectrum of the N/S-CDs after addition of Al3+. The absorption in the spectrum was enhanced possibly because of the chelation of the N/S-CDs with Al3+. FT-IR spectra of the N/S-CDs before and after addition of Al3+ were showed in Fig. 10(d) which revealed that the wavenumbers of multiple absorption peaks in the IR spectrum of the N/S-CDs varied after addition of Al3+. The spectral variations including the disappearance of original absorption bands and appearance of new absorption peaks were also observed. It was speculated that these changes were caused by the interactions between Al3+ and the multiple coordination sites on the surfaces of the N/S-CDs. The complexation resulted in the aggregation of the N/S-CDs and the incease of the particle size. Thus we proposed a plausible mechanism for the fluorescence enhancement of the N/S-CDs after addition of Al3+ based on the principle of AIEE as showed in Scheme 3 .

Owing to the significance of convenient and selective determination of Fe2+ and Al3+ ions, various chemical sensors had been constructed for the detection of Fe2+ and Al3+. Comparison of the performance including sensing mode or mechanism, applicable pH range, response time, and LOD of the N/S-CDs with other fluorescent chemosensors in documents for Fe2+ and Al3+ detection was summarized in Table 1 and Table 2 , respectively. Among the optical sensors for sensing Fe2+, only Rh-F [52] and Chemsensor 1 [55] could be used in a wide pH range of 2–13 and 1–12, respectively. However, both sensors responded to Fe2+ through the fluorescence quenching mode, which was susceptible to interference from background and other metal ions. It was seen from Table 2 that most fluorescent sensors for Al3+ detection could only be used in a narrow pH scale or even a specific pH value. Though sensor H2L displayed a wide applicable pH range of 5–11, the synthesis of the salamo-based probe involved several reaction steps and was time-consuming [63]. Probe SQ-BH also featured a broad applicable pH scale of 3–10 for Al3+ sensing, but the fluorescence enhancement of the squaraine-based probe caused by Al3+ was seriously interfered by Cu2+ and for Fe2+ [64]. It was concluded from the tables that compared with most fluorescent sensors, the N/S-CDs had the following comprehensive advantages: (1) Selective detection of Fe2+ without interference of Fe3+ by fluorescence turn-on and sensing of Al3+ by AIEE; (2) Steady detection of Fe2+ and Al3+ simultaneously in a wide pH range and prompt response to Al3+; (3) Lower detection limits.

In conclusion, carbon dots doped with nitrogen and sulfur (N/S-CDs) was synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. Characterization of the N/S-CDs by FT-IR spectra, TEM, XRD, XPS, zeta potential and particle size measurements demonstrated the existence of hydroxyl, amino and carboxyl groups on the surfaces of the N/S-CDs, which provided multiple active sites for interactions between the N/S-CDs and Fe2+ or Al3+. The N/S-CDs exhibited excellent stability in a broad pH range and high ionic strength. After addition of different metal ions including Li+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Al3+ and Fe3+ to the N/S-CDs, only Fe2+ and Al3+ could significantly enhance the fluorescence intensity and cause visible color change. The N/S-CDs responded to Al3+ quickly through the principle of AIEE and was applicable in a wide range of pH, indicating that the N/S-CDs could detect Al3+ with high selectivity and sensitivity and might be developed into luminous aggregate materials. At the same time, the N/S-CDs could detect Fe2+ by visual recognition and fluorescence turn-on dual-mode and interference from Fe3+ was excluded. The N/S-CDs showed relatively low detection limits, 6.99 nmol/L for Fe2+ and 5.50 nmol/L for Al3+, compared to most of other probes reported in literatures. Considering the vast amount of consumption of RB5 in dyestuff chemistry, this study may open a new door for resourcing RB5-containing printing and dyeing wastewater.

Yanxi Song: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Xiaofeng Xia: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – original draft. Zhongwen Xiao: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation. Yong Zhao: Methodology, Resources, Validation. Mengjie Yan: Investigation, Data curation. Jiayin Li: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Hongqi Li: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Xiaotian Liu: Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The Funding for the Open Research Program of State Key Laboratory of Molecular Engineering of Polymers, Fudan University (K2022-38 to Yanxi Song) is acknowledged.

Acta A 228 (2020) 117590.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence light up detection of aluminium ion and imaging in live cells based on the aggregation-induced emission enhancement of thiolated gold nanoclusters

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Luo, Y. Zheng, Z. Qin, C. Li, H. Jiang, X. Wang, Fluorescence light up detection of aluminium ion and imaging in live cells based on the aggregation-induced emission enhancement of thiolated gold nanoclusters, Talanta 204 (2019) 548–554.
